s: Open-air museum of Chicago's dead — architects, magnates, and Sullivan masterpieces
w: Architects buried here designed the city above ground; their tombs compete for glory below. Louis Sullivan carved the Getty Tomb in 1890 — a Romanesque elegy in limestone — and Burnham and Root sleep near the lake-view knoll. 121 acres of garden cemetery opened in 1860, every path a who's-who of the Gilded Age.
_facts: Established 1860 in Uptown Chicago.
Contains Getty Tomb designed by Louis Sullivan, 1890.
121 acres; burial site of Daniel Burnham, John Wellborn Root, and other notable Chicagoans.
On National Register of Historic Places.

s: Al Capone's favourite jazz dive — the tunnels are still there under your feet
w: Al Capone had his own booth and the tunnels ran south for a quick exit when the cops arrived — the story goes the rumrunner passages still honeycomb the floor. Jazz has played here since 1907, unbroken. The Sunday Uptown Poetry Slam has been held in this same room since 1986. Pull up a stool.
_facts: Jazz bar on Broadway in Uptown Chicago; operating since 1907.
Al Capone is said to have been a regular and to have had tunnels run under the club for escape.
Hosts the Uptown Poetry Slam, the longest-running poetry slam in the world, since 1986.
Listed on National Register of Historic Places.

s: John Dillinger walked out of here on July 22, 1934 — and never took another step
w: Public Enemy No. 1 had just watched Manhattan Melodrama when FBI agents shot him on the pavement outside. The 1915 movie house is a Chicago Landmark and National Historic Place; it now stages live theatre as the Victory Gardens Biograph, the ghost of that summer night still hovering over the marquee.
_facts: Built 1915 on Lincoln Avenue, Lincoln Park.
John Dillinger shot dead by FBI agents outside on July 22, 1934, after watching a gangster film.
Chicago Landmark since March 28, 2001; on National Register of Historic Places.
Now operates as the Victory Gardens Theater.

s: The oldest surviving high-rise in Chicago, and Frank Lloyd Wright rewired its light court
w: Burnham and Root completed this twelve-storey iron-and-masonry hybrid in 1888 — the oldest standing high-rise in the city. Two decades later Frank Lloyd Wright remodelled the central light court in white marble and gilt. Stand under the atrium and count the layers of time. National Historic Landmark.
_facts: Completed 1888, designed by Burnham and Root; oldest surviving high-rise in Chicago.
12 stories; 209 South LaSalle Street in the Loop.
Frank Lloyd Wright renovated the light court atrium in 1905.
National Historic Landmark; Chicago Landmark.

s: Louis Sullivan's very last building — all his dying genius compressed into a shopfront
w: Sullivan designed 126 buildings; this 1922 shopfront on Lincoln Avenue was number 126. He died penniless two years later. The terracotta facade bursts with his signature organic ornament — vines and geometric bursts framing a modest doorway. One last act, quietly placed in a German neighbourhood far from the Loop.
_facts: Built 1922 at 4611 North Lincoln Avenue; the last of 126 buildings designed by Louis Sullivan.
Sullivan died in 1924, destitute.
National Historic Landmark and Chicago Landmark.
Originally a music store for William P. Krause.

s: Czech immigrants built this opera house in 1892; it never stopped being used
w: John Dusek, a Bohemian tavern-keeper, built this Italianate hall in 1892 for the neighbourhood's Czech immigrants — opera house, ballroom and market under one roof. A century of use left it scruffy but sound; a 2013 restoration returned it to live music. Chicago Landmark since 1989.
_facts: Built 1892 in Pilsen neighborhood by Bohemian immigrant John Dusek.
Italianate design; originally housed opera house, ballroom, and market.
Chicago Landmark October 25, 1989.
Restored 2013; now an active music venue and bar.

s: The city's old public library still has its Tiffany dome — now it's all free
w: The city's main library from 1897, this beaux-arts building was too beautiful to demolish when a bigger one was needed. Preston Bradley Hall has a 38-foot Tiffany glass dome. Today it is a free arts centre and the mayor's official reception room — no ticket required.
_facts: Opened 1897 as Chicago Public Library; transferred to arts use when Harold Washington Library Center opened in 1991.
Preston Bradley Hall features a 38-foot Tiffany Studios art-glass dome.
National Historic Landmark; operated by Chicago Department of Cultural Affairs.
Free admission; houses city's official mayoral reception venue.

s: H.H. Richardson's fortress mansion on the street where the Gilded Age lived
w: Prairie Avenue was Chicago's millionaire row in the 1880s, and H.H. Richardson designed this granite stronghold for farm-equipment magnate John Glessner in 1887. The sole intact house on what was the city's most fashionable block. One of Richardson's finest surviving works. National Historic Landmark.
_facts: Designed 1885-1886 by H.H. Richardson, completed 1887.
Located at 1800 S. Prairie Avenue; Glessner was a partner at Warder, Bushnell & Glessner.
National Historic Landmark since 1976; Chicago Landmark since 1970.
Now operated as a house museum on the formerly elite Prairie Avenue.

s: Chicago's oldest surviving train station headhouse — the trains left but the clock tower stayed
w: Built in 1883, Dearborn Station was one of six intercity rail terminals that made Chicago the hub of American travel. The last train departed May 1, 1971. The Romanesque Revival clock tower survived demolition pressure; the station anchors Printer's Row, its waiting rooms converted but its bones intact.
_facts: Built 1883 at Dearborn and Polk Streets; Chicago's oldest surviving train station.
Last served passengers on May 1, 1971 when Amtrak consolidated Chicago terminals.
On National Register of Historic Places.
Trackage and train sheds demolished; headhouse redeveloped as commercial space.

s: Chicago's biggest and oldest private cemetery — 16 Union generals, the Tiffany windows, Oscar Mayer
w: Founded 1859, Chicago's largest cemetery holds a US vice president, 16 Civil War generals, and the earthly remains of Oscar Mayer and Montgomery Ward. The 1914 mausoleum has Tiffany windows. The castellated Gothic entrance gate is on the National Register of Historic Places in its own right.
_facts: Chicago's largest cemetery at 350 acres; opened 1859.
Entrance gate designed by William W. Boyington; listed on National Register of Historic Places 1975.
Rosehill Mausoleum (1914) has Tiffany windows by Louis Comfort Tiffany.
Over 190,000 interments including 16 Union Civil War generals and a US vice president (Charles Gates Dawes).

s: The Chicago School at its purest — 1895 lobby mosaics narrate a Jesuit explorer's journals
w: Completed 1895 by Holabird & Roche. The lobby is the point: Tiffany glass mosaics and bronze bas-reliefs retell Pere Marquette's 1674 journey through what is now Chicago, commissioned to explain the building's name. A practical skyscraper wearing extraordinary art for a hat.
_facts: Completed 1895; designed by Holabird & Roche.
Lobby features Tiffany glass mosaics and bronze reliefs depicting Pere Marquette's 1674 exploration.
Chicago Landmark; National Historic Landmark.
Now owned by the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation.

s: A free independent research library since 1887 — anyone can walk in off the street
w: Walter Loomis Newberry left his fortune to found a humanities library open to anyone with a curiosity. Since 1887: no university affiliation, no membership fee, seven million items from Ptolemy's Geographia to Chicago street directories. The Romanesque palazzo on Walton Street is as magnificent as the collection.
_facts: Founded 1887 with a bequest from Chicago businessman Walter Loomis Newberry.
Independent humanities research library; free and open to the public.
Holds approximately 1.5 million volumes and 5 million manuscript pages.
Henry Ives Cobb-designed building; Chicago Landmark.

s: The only Latino museum in the US accredited by the American Alliance of Museums
w: Carlos Tortolero founded this in 1982 with 900 works and a borrowed gallery; the collection now exceeds 10,000 pieces spanning 3,000 years of Mexican and Chicano culture. In Harrison Park, Pilsen, free since day one. The only Latino museum in the US accredited by the American Alliance of Museums.
_facts: Founded 1982 by Carlos Tortolero; opened to public March 27, 1987 in Pilsen.
Only Latino museum accredited by the American Alliance of Museums.
Permanent collection of over 10,000 works; admission always free.
Self-described as the largest Latino cultural institution in the US.

n: Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures Museum
s: The University of Chicago's ancient-world hoard — a Mesopotamian lion, the Megiddo ivories
w: James Henry Breasted founded this in 1919 and led expeditions to Mesopotamia, Egypt, and Persia; the results filled a 1931 Gothic building on campus. Now renamed ISAC, it holds a 17-foot Assyrian bull from Khorsabad, Sumerian tablets, and a lamassu that would dwarf most living rooms.
_facts: Founded 1919 by Egyptologist James Henry Breasted; housed in 1931 building on University of Chicago campus.
Renamed Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures (ISAC) in 2022, formerly the Oriental Institute.
Holdings include a 40,000-square-foot museum of ancient Near Eastern, Egyptian, Nubian, Anatolian, and Persian artifacts.
Khorsabad Court contains massive Assyrian bull colossi from the palace of Sargon II (721-705 BCE).

s: Where Toto and the Tin Man live in a neighbourhood park, honouring Chicago's own L. Frank Baum
w: L. Frank Baum wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz while living in Chicago, and this Lincoln Park neighbourhood claimed him. Bronze statues of Dorothy, the Tin Man, the Scarecrow, and the Cowardly Lion were installed between 1995 and 2007. The Emerald Garden vegetable plot occupies one corner. Thoroughly, sincerely absurd.
_facts: Named for L. Frank Baum, who lived in Chicago when he wrote The Wonderful Wizard of Oz (1900).
Located at 2021 North Burling Street in Lincoln Park.
Bronze statues of Oz characters installed 1995-2007 by community fundraising.
Features an 'Emerald Garden' community vegetable garden.

s: The only grave left in Lincoln Park — the city couldn't move it in 1864
w: When the city converted its old City Cemetery into Lincoln Park in 1864, every body was relocated — except Ira Couch, the hotel magnate, whose heirs held firm. The 1857 granite mausoleum still stands in the park, surrounded by joggers and dog-walkers who largely ignore the dead man in their midst.
_facts: Mausoleum built 1857 for hotelier Ira Couch; Tremont House owner.
When Lincoln Park was established from City Cemetery in 1864, all other remains were relocated; the Couch family successfully resisted moving the mausoleum.
Listed on the National Register of Historic Places.
Chicago Landmark; sits incongruously among park facilities.

s: One of the oldest ethnic museums in the US, in Chicago's old Polish Downtown
w: Founded in 1935 when Milwaukee Avenue was still densely Polish, this collection covers the diaspora from the partitioned homeland to South Chicago's steel mills. Paderewski's piano is here; so are WWII exile-government artefacts, folk costumes, and maps of a Poland that periodically ceased to exist.
_facts: Founded 1935 in the old Polish Downtown neighbourhood of Chicago.
One of the oldest ethnic museums in the United States.
Collection includes Ignacy Jan Paderewski memorabilia, WWII Polish exile-government artefacts, and folk art.
Core Member of Chicago Cultural Alliance consortium of 25 ethnic museums.

s: One of only two churches Louis Sullivan ever designed — Czar Nicholas II paid for half of it
w: Russian immigrants wanted a Sullivan-designed church; Sullivan delivered this jewel in 1903. Tsar Nicholas II contributed 4,000 roubles — which explains the Byzantine undercurrent beneath Sullivan's organic ornament. One of only two churches the father of the skyscraper designed. Chicago Landmark.
_facts: Designed by Louis Sullivan, completed 1903; one of only two churches he designed.
Tsar Nicholas II donated 4,000 roubles toward construction.
Cathedral of the Orthodox Church in America Diocese of the Midwest.
Listed on National Register of Historic Places; Chicago Landmark.

n: DuSable Black History Museum and Education Center
s: The museum that started in a living room in 1961 is now housed in a former Civil War armory
w: Margaret Taylor Burroughs founded this in her South Side living room in 1961 with 'a card table and some folding chairs.' It now holds 15,000-plus objects in a 1910 armory in Washington Park. Named for Jean Baptiste Pointe DuSable, the Haitian-born first non-indigenous settler of Chicago.
_facts: Founded 1961 by Margaret Taylor Burroughs and Charles G. Burroughs in their home.
Now located in a 1910 park fieldhouse/armory at 740 East Administration Drive in Washington Park.
Named for Jean Baptiste Pointe DuSable, the Haitian-born founder of the settlement that became Chicago.
Holds over 15,000 objects dedicated to African and African-American history and culture.

s: Nine Tiffany windows and an Arts and Crafts interior rebuilt after the fire of 1900
w: The congregation formed in 1842; the building opened 1874, burned 1900. The interior was rebuilt entirely in Arts and Crafts style — hand-craftsmanship, unity of design, nine Tiffany windows. One of the finest unaltered Arts and Crafts church interiors in the United States. National Historic Landmark since 2013.
_facts: Gothic Revival church at 1936 South Michigan Avenue; congregation founded 1842.
Interior rebuilt in Arts and Crafts style after 1900 fire.
Nine Tiffany Studios stained-glass windows in the sanctuary.
National Historic Landmark 2013; Chicago Landmark 1977.

s: Where Jane Addams invented the settlement house movement — and the idea of social work
w: Jane Addams and Ellen Gates Starr opened Hull House in 1889 for the immigrant poor. By 1911 it had grown to thirteen buildings providing childcare, education, arts, and labour advocacy. Addams won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1931. Two original buildings survive on the UIC campus, preserved as Addams left them.
_facts: Co-founded 1889 by Jane Addams and Ellen Gates Starr for recent European immigrants.
Grew from one house to 13 buildings by 1911; credited with inventing the settlement house movement.
Jane Addams was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1931.
Two original buildings survive; National Historic Landmark.

s: A Japanese garden that survived the 1893 World's Fair, two world wars, and decades of neglect
w: Japan built this garden on Wooded Island for the 1893 World's Fair; its Ho-o-den pavilion was the first authentic Japanese building most Americans had seen. The pavilion burned in the 1940s; the island survives, renamed Osaka Garden in 2013 after a torii gate arrived from the sister city. An islet of calm.
_facts: Japanese garden on Wooded Island in Jackson Park; originally created for 1893 World's Columbian Exposition.
Ho-o-den pavilion was one of the first authentic Japanese buildings built in the United States.
Renamed Osaka Garden in 2013 following a sister-city gift from Osaka.
The Ho-o-den pavilion burned in the 1940s; the island and landscaping survive.

s: A man-made limestone peninsula jutting into Lake Michigan — the best skyline shot in the city
w: The WPA built this landfill peninsula in the late 1930s and ringed it with a limestone revetment matching the park's Prairie aesthetic. On a clear day the Loop rises out of the lake to the north; to the south, nothing but water and sky. Chicago Landmark since 2023. The fireworks crowd knows.
_facts: Manmade peninsula on Lake Michigan in Burnham Park, Hyde Park; constructed from landfill in late 1930s.
Limestone revetment designed and built by Chicago Park District engineers.
Chicago Landmark since April 19, 2023.
Known as a premier spot for viewing the city skyline and Independence Day fireworks.

s: Mies van der Rohe's perfect glass box — 'the clearest structure we have ever done'
w: Mies designed this for his own architecture school at IIT, completed 1956. He called it 'the clearest structure we have ever done,' and he meant it: four exposed steel plate girders hold the roof, the ground floor is one unobstructed room. National Historic Landmark. Pilgrimage site for architects.
_facts: Designed by Ludwig Mies van der Rohe for Illinois Institute of Technology's College of Architecture, completed 1956.
Single-space glass and steel structure at 3360 South State Street.
Mies led IIT's architecture program for two decades; Crown Hall was its culmination.
National Historic Landmark; Chicago Landmark.

s: A 1929 atmospheric cinema that still has clouds drifting across a plaster night sky
w: Built 1929 in the 'atmospheric' era — theatres designed to feel like open-air courtyards under a starlit sky. The plaster ceiling still has its fake stars and drifting clouds. Continuously art-house since the early 1980s, best midnight double bills in Chicago. The twinkling lights never got turned off.
_facts: Built 1929 on North Southport Avenue in Lakeview.
'Atmospheric' movie palace designed to resemble an open-air courtyard under a night sky with real twinkling lights.
Operating continuously as art-house and repertory cinema since early 1980s.
Hosts annual 24-hour horror marathon and regular midnight screenings.

s: The room where Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, and the Rolling Stones all cut tracks
w: Leonard and Phil Chess — Polish-Jewish immigrants from Motol — ran the most influential blues label in America from this building on Motor Row between 1957 and 1967. Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf, Chuck Berry, and Bo Diddley all recorded here; in 1964 the Rolling Stones paid homage and cut '2120 South Michigan Ave.' Named for the address. Willie Dixon's Blues Heaven Foundation now keeps the building.
_facts: Chess Records occupied 2120 S. Michigan Avenue from May 1957 to 1967.
Label recorded Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, Howlin' Wolf, Bo Diddley, and Etta James.
The Rolling Stones recorded an instrumental tribute '2120 South Michigan Ave.' at this address in 1964.
Now home to Willie Dixon's Blues Heaven Foundation, a nonprofit preserving Chicago blues history.

s: The newspaper that launched the Great Migration — Pullman porters smuggled it south
w: Robert S. Abbott founded the Chicago Defender here in 1905 and turned it into the most influential Black newspaper in the country. Pullman porters carried it secretly by rail into the Jim Crow South, where it exhorted readers to come north. Each copy passed through four to five pairs of hands. The Great Migration followed.
_facts: Chicago Defender founded 1905 by Robert S. Abbott; building at 3435 South Indiana Avenue in Bronzeville.
Pullman porters surreptitiously distributed the paper to African-American readers throughout the segregated South.
Each copy estimated to be read by four to five people at its peak circulation.
Paper campaigned against Jim Crow violence and actively encouraged the Great Migration northward.

s: All that remains of the yards that made Chicago 'hog butcher to the world'
w: For 106 years from 1865, more meat passed through this patch of the South Side than anywhere else on earth. At its 1920s peak the yards employed 40,000 people and processed 18 million animals a year — the inspiration for Upton Sinclair's The Jungle. The limestone gate, built in 1879, survived everything. The cattle market did not.
_facts: Union Stock Yards operated 1865-1971 on the South Side's New City community area.
At peak in 1924, processed more animals than any other facility in the world.
Upton Sinclair researched and set The Jungle (1906) in these yards, exposing meatpacking conditions.
The 1879 limestone gate is a Chicago Landmark; the rest of the yards were closed and demolished.

s: An Art Deco jewel stranded alone on the West Side — terracotta at its most lavish
w: Meyer & Cook designed this exuberant 1928 bank when Austin was a thriving commercial hub. The Northwestern Terra Cotta Company covered every surface — geometric friezes, floral medallions, stylised eagles. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2023, decades after the neighbourhood's fortunes changed. The building outlasted the bank by a century.
_facts: Built 1928 at 5200 W. Chicago Avenue in Chicago's Austin community.
Designed by architects Meyer & Cook; terracotta ornament by Northwestern Terra Cotta Company.
Chicago Landmark; added to National Register of Historic Places in 2023.
Considered a rare intact example of Art Deco commercial architecture on the West Side.

s: 4,381-seat ghost palace — the largest surviving movie theatre in Chicago, dark since 1981
w: Rapp and Rapp designed this in 1925 for the Balaban & Katz chain, and its interior volume surpasses Radio City Music Hall. It seats 4,381 — more than any other Chicago movie house — yet has stood empty since 1981, a decaying Tudor-Spanish fantasia at Lawrence and Broadway. Every restoration plan stalls. The building endures.
_facts: Built 1925; designed by Rapp and Rapp for the Balaban & Katz chain.
At 4,381 seats, the largest surviving movie palace in Chicago; interior volume exceeds Radio City Music Hall.
Chicago Landmark since 1991; on National Register of Historic Places since 1986.
Closed 1981; repeatedly proposed for restoration but remains empty as of 2026.

s: Where the Ferris Wheel debuted and the word 'midway' entered the language in 1893
w: For the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition this mile-long corridor between Washington and Jackson Parks hosted the amusement zone, including George Ferris's first-ever Ferris Wheel, which carried 1.5 million passengers. So successful was the Midway's carnival section that 'midway' became the English word for the amusement strip at any fair, circus, or amusement park. Now a quiet green boulevard.
_facts: Originally designed by Olmsted, Vaux & Co. in the 1870s to connect Washington and Jackson Parks.
Served as the entertainment zone of the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition.
George Ferris's original Ferris Wheel debuted here in 1893, carrying over 1.5 million passengers.
The Midway's amusement section gave English the general term 'midway' for a carnival or fairground.

s: A Prairie School landscape hidden in Lincoln Park — most visitors walk straight past it
w: Alfred Caldwell redesigned this Edwardian lily pond in 1936-38 as a Prairie School landscape — rough limestone ledges, native plants, a council ring of stones. He called it 'the valley of the Midwest.' It sits just inside the park fence, overlooked by most passers-by. National Historic Landmark since 2006.
_facts: Redesigned 1936-38 by landscape architect Alfred Caldwell; originally an Edwardian pool.
Located at 125 W. Fullerton Parkway (between Stockton and Cannon Drives) in Lincoln Park.
National Historic Landmark since February 17, 2006; Chicago Landmark since 2002.
Example of Prairie School landscape architecture; features limestone boulders, native plants, and a council ring.

s: Chicago's oldest German-American club — formed in 1865 to sing at Lincoln's funeral
w: The Germania Club was founded in 1865 as a male choral society with a specific purpose: to sing at Abraham Lincoln's funeral. Germans were then Chicago's largest ethnic group. The five-storey 1889 building, with its grand ballroom and neoclassical-Renaissance facade, is the last major monument of their presence in the city. Chicago Landmark and National Register property.
_facts: Germania Club founded 1865 as the Germania Männerchor, specifically to sing at Abraham Lincoln's funeral.
Club building at 108 W. Germania Place constructed 1889; designed by August Fiedler.
Germans were once the largest ethnic community in Chicago.
Chicago Landmark (2011); on National Register of Historic Places since 1976.

s: The museum dedicated to self-taught art — including a recreation of Henry Darger's bedsit
w: Founded in 1991, Intuit is one of only a handful of museums worldwide devoted to outsider and self-taught art. Its prize possession is a recreation of Henry Darger's Lincoln Avenue room — the reclusive janitor who died in 1973 leaving 15,145 pages of illustrated fantasy novel no one had read. His neighbours threw it all away. An archivist saved it.
_facts: Founded 1991 in West Town; one of few museums internationally devoted to outsider and self-taught art.
Holds a recreation of the studio of Henry Darger, a reclusive Chicago janitor who created a vast secret body of art.
Darger died in 1973; his 15,145-page illustrated manuscript was discovered by neighbours and nearly destroyed.
Located at 756 N. Milwaukee Avenue; free on certain days.

s: Theaster Gates bought this derelict bank for one dollar — and filled it with Johnson Publishing archives
w: The Stony Island Trust and Savings Bank went dark in the 1980s; artist Theaster Gates acquired it from the city in 2013 for one dollar and funded restoration by selling marble fragments from the building as art objects. Inside: the complete Johnson Publishing archive (Ebony, Jet), a glass lantern slide collection, and 60,000 vinyl records. South Shore has rarely seen anything like it.
_facts: Originally built as a South Shore community bank; abandoned since the 1980s.
Theaster Gates acquired the building from the City of Chicago for one dollar in 2013.
Gates funded restoration partly by selling marble fragments from the building as art objects.
Houses the Johnson Publishing archive (Ebony and Jet magazines), a glass lantern slide collection, and 60,000 vinyl records.

s: Where a bomb in 1886 killed seven police — and created May Day as a global labour holiday
w: On May 4, 1886, a labour rally for the eight-hour day turned into a massacre when an unknown person threw a dynamite bomb into the police line, killing seven officers and at least four workers. Eight anarchists were tried; four hanged. The affair scandalised the world and inspired May 1st as International Workers' Day. Mary Brogger's 2004 bronze commemorates both sides.
_facts: Bombing at Haymarket Square on May 4, 1886 during a labour rally for the eight-hour work day.
Seven police officers and at least four civilians killed; dozens wounded.
Eight men convicted on disputed evidence; four executed, one commuted, three eventually pardoned.
The event directly inspired May 1 as International Workers' Day in most of the world.

s: A Shriners' Moorish fantasy that is now — briefly — Chicago's first casino
w: The Medinah Shriners built this onion-domed extravaganza in 1912 as a ceremonial hall and auditorium. For decades it hosted the Shrine Circus and the Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey. After years as a Bloomingdale's, it became a Chicago Landmark in 2000 and in 2023 opened as Bally's temporary casino while the permanent one is built nearby. Stranger journeys are rare.
_facts: Built 1912 for the Medinah Shrine temple; architects Huehl & Schmid in Moorish Revival style.
Hosted the Shrine Circus and Ringling Bros. and Barnum & Bailey circus for decades.
Chicago Landmark since 2000; converted to Bloomingdale's home-furnishings store before casino use.
Opened as Bally's Chicago casino (temporary location) in 2023 while the permanent casino is under construction.

s: A Gilded Age banker's mansion restored to astonishing excess — free on certain Tuesdays
w: Samuel Mayo Nickerson, one of Chicago's great Gilded Age bankers, built this 1883 mansion and filled it with marble, onyx, and carved woodwork of almost oppressive richness. Financier Richard Driehaus restored it to museum use, interpreting the taste of the aesthetic moment rather than the family itself. Every surface is a statement.
_facts: Built 1883 for banker Samuel Mayo Nickerson; 50 East Erie Street, Near North Side.
Restored and opened as museum by philanthropist Richard H. Driehaus in 2008.
Interprets Gilded Age design, Art Nouveau, and the decorative arts of the era.
National Historic Landmark (as Nickerson Mansion); Chicago Landmark.

s: The only building both Sullivan and Wright worked on together — and they disagreed about who designed it
w: Louis Sullivan took the commission in 1891; his apprentice Frank Lloyd Wright claimed he did most of the design work. Both names clung to it for over a century. The result — simple, flat planes, no Victorian fussiness — was years ahead of its time. Now headquarters of the Society of Architectural Historians, open for tours. National Historic Landmark.
_facts: Completed 1892 at 1365 North Astor Street for lumber magnate James Charnley.
Designed by Louis Sullivan of Adler & Sullivan with significant involvement of apprentice Frank Lloyd Wright.
Both Sullivan and Wright later claimed primary authorship of the design.
Now owned by the Society of Architectural Historians; National Historic Landmark; Chicago Landmark.

s: A whites-only country club the city bought in 1975 — Obama's wedding reception was here
w: The South Shore Country Club refused Jewish and Black members even as its neighbourhood changed; by 1973 it folded. The Chicago Park District bought the Mediterranean Revival complex for $9.775 million, saved it from demolition, and reopened it as a cultural centre. Barack and Michelle Obama held their wedding reception here on October 3, 1992. The Blues Brothers used the ballroom exterior. Public beach, open stables.
_facts: Founded 1905 as the South Shore Country Club; originally Protestant-only, later restricted Jewish and Black membership.
Sold to Chicago Park District in 1975 for $9.775 million after membership declined with neighbourhood integration.
Barack and Michelle Obama held their wedding reception here on October 3, 1992.
Used as the 'Palace Hotel Ballroom' exterior in The Blues Brothers (1980).

s: 149 stones from the world's great monuments are mortared into the base — Parthenon, Taj Mahal, the Alamo
w: Tribune correspondents spent decades smuggling rocks home: a fragment from the Parthenon, a piece of the Taj Mahal, a sliver of the Great Wall, stones from Notre-Dame, Angkor Wat, the Alamo, Lincoln's Tomb, and Hagia Sophia are among 149 specimens mortared into the tower's lower facade. The neo-Gothic skyscraper opened in 1925 after a 1922 design competition that reshaped American architecture. WGN Radio broadcast from here for a century.
_facts: Designed by John Mead Howells and Raymond Hood; completed 1925 after an international design competition with over 260 entries.
Lower facade contains 149 stone fragments from historically significant sites worldwide, collected by Chicago Tribune correspondents.
Fragments include pieces from the Parthenon, Taj Mahal, Great Wall of China, Notre-Dame, Angkor Wat, and the Alamo.
WGN Radio (720 kHz) broadcast from the building from its opening until June 2018.

s: Where Charlie Chaplin made movies in 1915 — Gloria Swanson started here as an extra
w: George Spoor and Gilbert 'Broncho Billy' Anderson founded Essanay in Chicago in 1907 — the name from their initials, S and A. By 1915 Charlie Chaplin was their biggest star, making his Tramp films on Argyle Street. Gloria Swanson, Wallace Beery, and Ben Turpin also worked here. The founders won honorary Oscars in 1948 and 1958. The brick building now houses St. Augustine College.
_facts: Essanay Film Manufacturing Company founded 1907 by George Kirke Spoor and Gilbert M. Anderson.
Charlie Chaplin made his famous Tramp films here in 1915-16; Gloria Swanson and Wallace Beery also worked at the studio.
Studio name formed by phonetically spelling founders' initials S and A.
Founders Spoor and Anderson received honorary Academy Awards in 1948 and 1958 respectively for pioneering film contributions.

s: Chicago's oldest church — one of only a handful of buildings to survive the 1871 Great Fire
w: Built in 1854 and dedicated in 1856, this Romanesque brick church is the oldest standing church building in Chicago and one of a tiny handful of structures that survived the Great Fire of 1871. The twin towers honour East and West — one Romanesque, one Byzantine. It has been described as 'the cornerstone of Irish culture' in Chicago, though today it draws from across the city.
_facts: Church building constructed 1854-1856 at 700 West Adams Street; city's oldest standing church.
One of a handful of structures to survive the Great Chicago Fire of 1871.
Twin towers: one Romanesque, one Byzantine, representing East and West.
On National Register of Historic Places since 1977.

s: Where the world's first successful open-heart surgery was performed in 1893
w: Daniel Hale Williams founded Provident in 1891 as the first hospital in the United States open to patients and staff of all races. Two years later, on July 9, 1893, he operated on a man stabbed through the pericardium and sutured the heart — the first successful cardiac surgery in recorded history. The hospital survived, relocated, and is still operating today as a Cook County facility.
_facts: Founded 1891 by Daniel Hale Williams as the first non-segregated hospital in the United States.
On July 9, 1893, Dr. Daniel Hale Williams performed the first documented successful open-heart surgery at Provident.
Patient James Cornish survived a stab wound to the pericardium; Williams sutured it without antibiotics or modern equipment.
Hospital closed 1987-1993; reopened as Provident Hospital of Cook County at 550 E. 51st Street.
